title: Correcting Circular Reference Detection in Linked List

statement.markdown: Solve the problem and fix the bugs.
statement.outputFormat: Return true if the linked list contains a cycle, false otherwise.
statement.notes: Use Floyd's Tortoise and Hare algorithm, also known as the slow and fast pointer approach.

hints.text: Use the slow and fast pointer approach to find the cycle. If there is no cycle, the two pointers will eventually meet at a node (possibly the head of the list) and then move off the end.
If the fast pointer catches up to the slow pointer, then we have found a cycle in the list.
If the two pointers never meet, then we do not have a cycle in the list.

editorial.title: Correcting Circular Reference Detection in Linked List

solutions.python:
import sys
import unittest

class ListNode:
    def __init__(self, val=0, next=None):
        self.val = val
        self.next = next

class Solution:
    def hasCycle(self, head: ListNode) -> bool:
        if not head:
            return False
        slow = head
        fast = head
        while fast and fast.next:
            slow = slow.next
            fast = fast.next.next
            if slow == fast:
                return True
        return False

if __name__ == '__main__':
    input_lines = sys.stdin.read().splitlines()
    input_str = ' '.join(input_lines)
    l = input_str.split(' -> ')
    n = int(l[0])
    head = ListNode(int(l[1]))
    curr = head
    for i in range(2, 1 + n):
        curr.next = ListNode(int(l[i]))
        curr = curr.next
    if l[1 + n] != l[1]:
        cycle_node = l.index(l[1 + n])
        curr = head
        for i in range(1, cycle_node):
            curr = curr.next
        curr.next = head
    else:
        curr = head

    solution = Solution()
    result = solution.hasCycle(head)
    result_str = 'True' if result else 'False'
    sys.stdout.write(result_str + '
')
